1: YHWH spoke to Moses and Aaron — both Moses and Aaron are addressed because the discharge legislation (Lev 15) involves priestly oversight of offerings (Leviticus 15:14-15,29-30) and priestly protection from defilement (Leviticus 15:31); it is both prophetic legislation and priestly practice.
2: Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, when any man has a discharge from his body (zov mibsaro), his discharge is unclean — zov (flow, discharge) is the root term for the entire chapter; the zav (man with chronic discharge) and zavah (woman with irregular discharge) are named after it. The discharge renders the man (zav) himself impure, not merely the discharge substance.
3: And this is the law of his uncleanness for a discharge: whether his body runs with his discharge, or his body is blocked up by his discharge, it is his uncleanness — the distinction between a running (chronic, active) discharge and a blocked one suggests both acute and chronic presentations of the condition; both create the same degree of impurity in the person himself.
4: Every bed on which the one with the discharge lies shall be unclean, and everything on which he sits shall be unclean — contact impurity spreads from the zav to surfaces he lies or sits on, creating a secondary level of impurity that contaminates anyone who subsequently contacts those surfaces (Leviticus 15:5). The impurity radiates outward from the person through physical contact points.
5: And anyone who touches his bed shall wash his clothes and bathe himself in water and be unclean until the evening — the standard purification formula: laundering + immersion + sunset. The erev (evening) boundary marks the natural daily reset point in the Israelite purity system; all contact impurities of ordinary persons resolve by sunset after purification.
6: And whoever sits on anything on which the one with the discharge has sat shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — secondary contact (sitting where the zav sat) creates the same temporary impurity as direct contact. The transmission chain: zav → surface → person → until-evening impurity.
7: And whoever touches the body of the one with the discharge shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — direct body contact with the zav also creates until-evening impurity with standard purification. The categories of contact in Leviticus 15:4-12 systematically cover: his bed, his seat, his body, his spittle, anything he rides, anything he touches, anything under him.
8: And if the man with the discharge spits on someone who is clean, then he shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — saliva from the zav transmits impurity; the specific mention of spitting indicates that any bodily emission from the zav can create contact impurity, not only the discharge itself.
9: And any saddle on which the one with the discharge rides shall be unclean — the saddle (or riding apparatus) is treated like a sitting surface (Leviticus 15:6); a riding animal's saddle contacts both the animal and the rider in extended proximity, warranting specific inclusion alongside beds and seats.
10: And whoever touches anything that was under him shall be unclean until the evening. And whoever carries such things shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — carrying objects that were under the zav creates a higher degree of contact than merely touching them, warranting laundering in addition to bathing; the distinction between touching and carrying reflects graduated impurity transmission based on degree of contact.
11: Anyone whom the one with the discharge touches without having rinsed his hands in water shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — if the zav has not washed his hands, touching another person transmits impurity; if he has washed his hands, touching does not transmit impurity. Hand-washing provides a practical limiter on the zav's social interaction, enabling some normal social life during the period of discharge.
12: And an earthen vessel that the one with the discharge touches shall be broken, and every vessel of wood shall be rinsed with water — earthenware cannot be purified (Leviticus 11:33; Leviticus 6:28; Leviticus 15:12) and must be destroyed; wooden vessels can be rinsed and remain in use. The distinction between earthenware and wood in impurity purifiability is a consistent principle throughout Leviticus (cf. Leviticus 11:32-35).
13: And when the one with a discharge is cleansed of his discharge, then he shall count for himself seven days for his cleansing, and wash his clothes. And he shall bathe his body in running water (mayim hayyim) and shall be clean — the chronic discharge requires seven clear days after cessation before purification is possible; running water (hayyim, living/flowing) is required for the zav's purification as for the metzora's (Leviticus 14:5-6), a higher standard than still water. The seven-day waiting period confirms that the discharge has truly ended.
14: And on the eighth day he shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons and come before YHWH to the entrance of the tent of meeting and give them to the priest — the eighth-day offering mirrors the metzora's eighth-day re-entry (Leviticus 14:10-20,23); both conditions require sacrifice on the day after a seven-day purification period. The two birds (one sin offering, one burnt offering, Leviticus 15:15) restore full sanctuary access.
15: And the priest shall use them, one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH for his discharge — the atonement formula (kipper alav lifney YHWH) for discharge, as for tzara'at, skin disease, and childbirth (Leviticus 12:7-8), indicates that these conditions are not treated as purely physical matters but as states requiring priestly intercession and sacrificial restoration.
16: If a man has an emission of semen (shichvat-zera), he shall bathe his whole body in water and be unclean until the evening — incidental semen emission (not the chronic condition of the zav) creates only until-evening impurity after bathing, without requiring the seven-day waiting period or the eighth-day sacrifice. The proportional response: chronic discharge → 7 days + offering; incidental emission → until evening + bathing.
17: And every garment and every skin on which the semen comes shall be washed with water and be unclean until the evening — the semen-stained garment or leather article requires laundering; the impurity affects objects as well as the person, but with the same minimal duration (until evening).
18: If a man lies with a woman and has an emission of semen, both of them shall bathe themselves in water and be unclean until the evening — sexual intercourse creates mutual until-evening impurity for both partners, requiring bathing for both. This provision is not a moral judgment on marital intercourse but a purity regulation that creates a temporary, resolvable state requiring the same basic purification.
19: When a woman has a discharge, and the discharge in her body is blood (vedam yihyeh zova bivisra), she shall be in her menstrual impurity (niddatah) for seven days — the woman's normal menstrual flow creates the niddah state for seven days; anyone who touches her is unclean until evening (Leviticus 15:19b). The term niddah (separation, impurity) names the state the woman enters during menstruation.
20: And everything on which she lies during her menstrual impurity shall be unclean. Everything also on which she sits shall be unclean — the niddah woman's bed and seat become secondary impurity sources, identical to the zav's furniture (Leviticus 15:4-6); the structural parallel between male and female discharge impurity is intentional, treating both conditions under a unified framework.
21: And whoever touches her bed shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — contact with the niddah woman's bed creates the standard until-evening impurity with laundering + bathing, identical to contact with the zav's bed (Leviticus 15:5).
22: And whoever touches anything on which she sits shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — the systematic coverage of bed and seat for the niddah parallels the coverage for the zav (Leviticus 15:5-6); the parallel legislation reflects deliberate structural symmetry, not two separate traditions merged together.
23: Whether it is the bed or anything on which she sits, when he touches it he shall be unclean until the evening — the summary generalizes from the specific examples; all surfaces on which the niddah has lain or sat create contact impurity. The evening boundary applies universally to this category of secondary contact impurity.
24: And if any man lies with her and her menstrual impurity comes upon him (vetihyeh niddatah alav), he shall be unclean seven days, and every bed on which he lies shall be unclean — sexual intercourse during the niddah period creates a seven-day impurity for the man (not merely until-evening), and his bed becomes an impurity source during that period. Intercourse during niddah is treated more seriously than contact with niddah furniture.
25: If a woman has a discharge of blood for many days, not at the time of her menstrual impurity, or if she has a discharge beyond the time of her impurity, all the days of the discharge she shall continue in uncleanness — the irregular or prolonged discharge beyond the normal menstrual period creates an extended impurity state. The woman of Mark 5:25-34 (who had a flow of blood for twelve years) would have been in continuous niddah impurity under this provision throughout the twelve years, excluded from temple participation and making her contact with Jesus culturally transgressive.
26: Every bed on which she lies, all the days of her discharge, shall be to her as the bed of her menstrual impurity, and everything on which she sits shall be unclean, as the uncleanness of her menstrual impurity — the zavah (woman with irregular discharge) generates the same secondary impurity as the niddah throughout the entire period of the discharge, which may extend indefinitely. Every day of discharge creates the same contamination of her sleeping and sitting surfaces.
27: Whoever touches these things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and be unclean until the evening — contact with the zavah's furniture creates the same until-evening impurity as contact with niddah furniture (Leviticus 15:21-22). The transmission rules for irregular discharge match those for normal menstruation.
28: But if she is cleansed of her discharge, she shall count for herself seven days, and after that she shall be clean — as with the zav (Leviticus 15:13), the zavah must count seven clear days after the discharge ends before she can be pronounced clean; the symmetry between male and female chronic discharge legislation is complete. After the seven days she proceeds to the eighth-day offering (Leviticus 15:29).
29: And on the eighth day she shall take two turtledoves or two pigeons and bring them to the priest, to the entrance of the tent of meeting — the zavah's eighth-day offering (two birds) mirrors the zav's offering (Leviticus 15:14) exactly; both undergo identical restoration procedures. The turtledoves/pigeons are the standard provision for those who cannot afford larger animals.
30: And the priest shall use one for a sin offering and the other for a burnt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for her before YHWH for her unclean discharge — the atonement formula for the zavah is word-for-word identical to the formula for the zav (Leviticus 15:15), completing the structural symmetry of male and female discharge legislation.
31: Thus you shall keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness (ve-hizzartem et-bney Yisra'el mituma'tam), lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle (mishkani) that is in their midst — the stated purpose of the entire discharge legislation: separation from impurity prevents the death that occurs when uncleanness contacts the holy sanctuary. The tabernacle's presence among the people creates both the gift of divine nearness and the requirement of purity; the two are inseparable.
32: This is the law for him who has a discharge and for him who has an emission of semen, becoming unclean thereby — the closing summary of male discharge legislation: the zav (chronic discharge) and the man with semen emission are both included under torat ha-zav.
33: And for her who is unwell with her menstrual impurity and for anyone, male or female, who has a discharge, and for the man who lies with a woman who is unclean — the closing summary explicitly names: (1) the niddah, (2) any person with discharge regardless of sex, and (3) the man who has intercourse with a niddah. The final phrase asher yishkav im-niddah (who lies with a niddah) identifies this as a distinct category requiring its own mention in the summary, consistent with the elevated impurity level of Leviticus 15:24.
